quantum mechanics starting from let's say qubits gates circuits in quantum computing and the concepts like superposition entanglement and interference in quantum mechanics now in week three we get to the interesting part about seeing how a quantum computer can leverage these principles to solve some certain problems faster than uh what a classic computer can do okay and there will be a two parts in week three first is the coverage of twice yosa algorithm and the second part will be the coverage of grover's algorithm i will be doing the first part on deutsche sur and and dina karen i'm a researcher in the ibm india research lab and i'm also ibm ambassador okay let's take a step back right so why would you want to study about quantum algorithms or what can be the main reason because we already have classical computers which can solve many interesting things and sometimes like beyond our imagination right so why are quantum computers interesting they're interesting only when they can do something which can be faster or it can provide a better quality solution than what any classical computer can provide right so when this research on quantum computing started in late 80s and early 90s the initial research was just people are curious about finding one problem for which quantum computers can do probably faster than any classical computer right initially they didn't really care about a real world application to be honest in 1990s uh even for classic computers there weren't tons of any uh they weren't any interesting real-world applications as we see today right back then people are just at least coming up are interested in coming up with some problem and uh for which quantum computers can do faster than classic computers and indeed the initial problems that were studied were toy problems and one more interesting point is that at that point they were not even a consensus whether quantum computers are faster than classical computers right in fact dan simon and this advisor did not believe that quantum computers are faster so simon set out to prove that quantum computers are not any better than classical computers and he found out an interesting problem for which quantum computers can be exponentially faster than classical computers in subset and indeed that formed a strong foundation for schar's algorithm which is a breakthrough for quantum computing because for the first time for a problem of real world interest it is shown that quantum computers can do much faster than the best known classical algorithm it's a schwarz algorithm for prime factorization where peter short showed that given a product of two numbers which is the basis of modern cryptography he showed that quantum computers can break that which means that given a product of two primes quantum computer can find the individual primes p and q but this forms the basis of rsa cryptosystem which assumes that it is hard for any classical computer to do this factorization and hence we have built cryptosystems on top of it and it's used widely right so charles algorithm gained a lot of attention and after that we have had uh many interesting relevant problems come up for which quantum computers can do faster so for instance this grover's algorithm for generic search uh which said that there is a quadratic improvement for quantum computers over classical computers and from 90s in the current decade or the previous decade we have started to see quantum algorithms for general optimization problems and in 2016 interesting thing happened that quantum computers are not just restricted to labs and a handful of people it's available to everyone to

# Week 3: Standard Algorithms

### Deutsch-Jozsa Algorithm

be used in cloud which ibm first introduced in 2016 for quantum computers and after that we have started seeing a plethora of concrete real-world use cases let's say in battery optimization or the finance industry for pricing some contracts or in general in quantum machine learning new research has started happening and the research results being shown for concrete real world problems in the last few years to start with we will focus on the diet show sub problem and give a brief glimpse of continuous ironic problem as i mentioned these are type problems but this gives a good idea of the fundamental building blocks the quantum algorithms use in the primitive stages and then how these will get built they will start seeing one by one in the next few lectures okay before we go into the algorithm let's first try to understand what does it mean for a quantum computer to be faster than a classical computer how do we say that this is fast and this is probably slower to solve the specific task let us now look at different ways of measuring the performance or what is called the complexity of algorithms the most natural one is called computation complexity which measures the total computation time taken by the algorithm to solve the problem we can also measure space complexity to capture the maximum space or memory ramp storage that's required by the algorithm at any point of time for distributed algorithms which involve computation between different clusters communication complex information complexity can also be used but to differentiate uh the complexity of two different notions of computing query complexity seems more natural so query complexity what it says is that these algorithms they also have access to a black box called an article which has a function embedded in it and whenever this algorithm that we are interested in it wants to to solve that specific uh function it can just provide an uh query to this article and get back an answer so this is a theoretical abstraction that is used to easily differentiate between two different kinds of algorithms okay so to recap a query complexity of an algorithm is time taken by the main module of an algorithm plus the number of times it calls the oracle so this abstract notion i mean just example the oracle can be just as simple as computing the logarithm function or it can be solving a linear program it says that i mean given a set of constraints you find an input such that some subset of constraints are minimized on that uh set of values or it can be more powerful like a prime factorization or it can even be something like solving a np complete problem okay but the main usage of this article is to of of this abstraction of oracle is to differentiate the performance of two different functions or classes of functions which are hard to differentiate by themselves by measuring the time okay this is just to provide an illustration later will go into the deutsches algorithm there we'll see it more detail but here let's say for example right uh we want to solve a system of linear equations like given a set of equations we want to provide get a solution for it if we have access to this linear program solver oracle you just make one query to this and then solve right so the complexity of this is unit time in contrast let's say we want to solve the graph coloring uh problem which is an np complete problem so the best known algorithm for now it takes exponential time to solve this even if we have access to this powerful enough oracle of solving linear programs okay so to summaries query complexity is uh the complexity that's used to separate the performance of two different classes of functions or complexity of two classes of algorithms and this is a natural model that researchers have used to show that quantum computers are more powerful than classical computing okay so now this is the background ah these are all the background that we

particular problem which for all inputs quantum computers can solve with certainty in polynomial time but classical computers will take exponential time if they want to solve with hundred percent
